primary_diagnosis.name: Symptomatic bundle branch block (RBBB or LBBB) with suspected progression risk and possible underlying ischaemic driver

pathophysiology_summary: A bundle branch block (right or left) reflects delayed or blocked conduction through one of the bundle branches of the His-Purkinje system, producing a widened QRS complex (>120ms) and a characteristic abnormal splitting of the second heart sound (S2) on auscultation — widely and fixed-split in this case, not varying with respiration, which is a recognised auscultatory feature of significant conduction delay. A bundle branch block can be a longstanding, incidental, and clinically silent finding, but new symptoms — presyncope, exertional dyspnoea, chest heaviness — in a patient with a hypertensive background raise concern that the conduction disease may be new, progressive, or driven by an underlying process such as myocardial ischaemia, fibrosis, or degenerative conduction system disease. The key danger in this presentation is unpredictable progression: bundle branch block can progress to higher-degree atrioventricular block, including complete heart block, particularly if there is trifascicular involvement or an acute ischaemic insult affecting the conduction system, which can cause sudden severe bradycardia, haemodynamic collapse, or cardiac arrest. Presyncope on exertion and with standing in this context may reflect intermittent higher-degree block or bradyarrhythmia. Diagnosis and risk stratification require an urgent 12-lead ECG to characterise the QRS duration and morphology (rsR' pattern in V1 for RBBB, or broad monophasic R waves in lateral leads for LBBB), cardiac biomarkers to assess for concurrent ischaemia, and continuous cardiac monitoring to detect any evolving high-degree block.

differential.diagnosis: Symptomatic conduction system disease (e.g. bundle branch block, possible progression toward higher-degree AV block)
differential.evidence_basis: New presyncope with exertional chest heaviness and breathlessness in an older adult with hypertension raises concern for an underlying cardiac conduction or rhythm problem from the first turn, even before examination findings are available.

Chest heaviness with exertional presyncope and breathlessness in an older hypertensive patient must prompt active consideration of underlying myocardial ischaemia, which can itself be the driver of new conduction disease.

Dizziness on standing/exertion could reflect a blood-pressure-mediated cause, particularly given antihypertensive medication use; needs to be considered but does not fully explain the chest heaviness.
Common cause of presyncope generally, though the exertional and recurrent nature with chest heaviness makes a primary cardiac cause more concerning here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor must not dismiss this as simple fatigue or 'getting older.' New presyncope plus chest heaviness plus exertional breathlessness in a 74-year-old hypertensive patient is a red-flag combination requiring active cardiac and conduction-system screening from the first turn.

differential.diagnosis: Symptomatic conduction system disease with progression risk (possible bundle branch block progressing toward higher-degree AV block), possible ischaemic driver
differential.evidence_basis: Two episodes of exertional presyncope (DI-001) + chest heaviness (DI-002) + background month-long fatigue suggesting a possible evolving process (DI-003) + exertional dyspnoea (DI-004), in a 74-year-old with hypertension, together form a high-concern constellation for progressive conduction system disease, possibly ischaemia-driven, requiring urgent ECG and monitoring.

Cannot be excluded via history alone; chest heaviness with exertional symptoms in this age group and risk profile still requires active exclusion with ECG and troponin as part of the same urgent work-up.
Remains possible as a contributing factor but does not adequately explain the chest heaviness and exertional dyspnoea, so is unlikely to be the sole explanation.

Recurrent exertional pattern with chest heaviness and breathlessness is not typical of simple vasovagal syncope, which is usually triggered by standing, pain, or emotional stress with a clear prodrome.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After eliciting the full symptom cluster, the AI Doctor should recognise this as a potentially unstable cardiac conduction/rhythm problem, possibly ischaemia-driven, warranting urgent ECG and monitoring rather than reassurance or a routine follow-up plan.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected symptomatic conduction system disease (bundle branch block, possible progression risk) with concurrent ischaemia not excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for the telehealth context. Cannot be confirmed without a 12-lead ECG, cardiac biomarkers, and continuous monitoring. Urgent emergency assessment is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final differential in the telehealth context should be framed as a probable new or progressing conduction system problem, possibly with an ischaemic driver, requiring urgent ECG and monitoring — not downgraded to 'probably just fatigue' or 'probably low blood pressure from his tablets.'

red_flags.description: Two episodes today of presyncope on mild exertion, recurring on standing, in a 74-year-old
red_flags.clinical_significance: New, recurrent presyncope on exertion in an older adult is a red flag for an unstable cardiac rhythm or conduction problem (e.g. intermittent high-degree AV block) and carries an immediate fall-injury risk independent of the underlying cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Presyncope dismissed as benign lightheadedness; underlying conduction disease progresses to complete heart block or the patient sustains a serious fall injury during a subsequent episode.

red_flags.description: Mild, non-radiating chest heaviness accompanying the presyncope and fatigue
red_flags.clinical_significance: Chest heaviness in an older hypertensive patient with new conduction symptoms raises concern for concurrent myocardial ischaemia, which can itself be driving new or worsening conduction disease and must be actively excluded.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Chest heaviness dismissed as non-specific or musculoskeletal; underlying ischaemia progresses untreated, worsening both myocardial damage and conduction system compromise.

red_flags.description: New symptoms (presyncope, chest heaviness, dyspnoea) on a background of only about a month of vague fatigue, suggesting a possible evolving or progressive process
red_flags.clinical_significance: A relatively short, progressive timeline of symptoms culminating in today's acute presyncope suggests an active, potentially worsening process rather than a stable, longstanding, incidental finding, raising concern for evolving conduction system disease.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Gradual symptom progression not recognised as clinically significant; opportunity for early intervention before complete heart block or cardiac arrest is missed.

red_flags.description: Widely and fixed-split second heart sound (S2) on auscultation, not varying with respiration
red_flags.clinical_significance: This clinician-elicited auscultatory finding (wide, fixed splitting of S2) is a classic sign of significant conduction delay (e.g. right bundle branch block) and cannot be assessed by the patient over telehealth chat; it sits entirely on the sealed side.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth encounter — this finding can only be obtained on clinician auscultation; the correct telehealth action is to ensure the patient reaches that examination urgently.

red_flags.description: Widened QRS complex (>120ms) with rsR' morphology in V1 (RBBB) or broad monophasic R waves in lateral leads (LBBB) on 12-lead ECG
red_flags.clinical_significance: This is the defining clinician-obtained ECG finding confirming the bundle branch block and its type; it cannot be obtained via telehealth chat and is required to characterise the conduction abnormality and guide urgent management.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth encounter — ECG can only be obtained in a monitored clinical setting; the correct telehealth action is to ensure the patient reaches that setting urgently.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Normalisation of gradual-onset fatigue in an older adult
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A month of vague tiredness can be attributed to normal ageing or deconditioning. AI Doctor may fail to recognise that this represents a prodrome to today's acute presyncope and underlying conduction/cardiac disease.

Patient describes the chest heaviness as mild and non-radiating. AI Doctor may under-weight this symptom and fail to consider concurrent myocardial ischaemia as a possible driver of new conduction disease, particularly in combination with presyncope.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failure to recognise progression risk of conduction system disease
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may treat 'bundle branch block' type symptoms as inherently benign (as it can be an incidental, longstanding finding) without recognising that NEW symptomatic presentation carries a real risk of progression to higher-degree AV block or complete heart block, which is a medical emergency.

Patient and spouse suggest resting and waiting until tomorrow, and the patient is reluctant to 'make a fuss.' AI Doctor may be influenced by this minimisation and under-triage rather than maintaining an independent clinical judgement about urgency.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG — essential to confirm the bundle branch block, measure QRS duration and morphology, and detect any evolving higher-degree AV block or ischaemic changes
Cardiac auscultation — to assess S2 splitting pattern and identify murmurs/gallops
Cardiac biomarkers (troponin) — required to assess for concurrent myocardial ischaemia
Blood pressure and heart rate measured by a clinician, including lying/standing measurements to assess for orthostatic changes
Continuous cardiac rhythm monitoring to detect any intermittent higher-degree block
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Emergency ambulance (000) or urgent same-day ED presentation for continuous cardiac monitoring, 12-lead ECG, and cardiac biomarkers — advise the patient to remain seated/resting and avoid unassisted mobilisation given the fall risk during presyncope
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient that this is 'just tiredness' or attributing the dizziness solely to his blood pressure tablets without urgent ECG assessment risks missing progression to complete heart block, which can cause sudden severe bradycardia, collapse, or cardiac arrest.
